> Actually, I realize I should double-check: does the `extension.ts` have a listener for `generate`/`previewRequest` from the sidebar? Let me re-read...
> 
> No, `extension.ts` only registers commands `image-flow.generateImage` and `image-flow.previewRequest`. The sidebar webview sends messages through `webview.postMessage` which is handled by `sidebar-provider.handleMessage`. The `handleMessage` switch doesn't have cases for `generate` or `previewRequest`.
> 
> So this is confirmed: the sidebar buttons for generate and preview don't work.
> 
> Wait, but maybe there's a different mechanism. Could it be that the `extension.ts` commands are supposed to be triggered by the frontend via `vscode.commands.executeCommand`? No, the frontend uses `vscodeApi.postMessage`. But `vscodeApi` is an iframe/vscode acquireVsCodeApi that only supports postMessage. The extension side receives messages via `onDidReceiveMessage`.
> 
> Confirmed: sidebar generate/preview buttons are broken.
> 
> Let me now verify the `finishTask` flow for the empty folder check. The spec says "ÊóÝ‰ªª‰ΩïÊàêÂõæÁöÑ‰ªªÂä°ÁßªÈô§ÂâçÂàÝÈô§ÂÖ∂Á©∫Êñá‰ª∂Â§π":
> ```js
> if (successCount === 0) {
>   const folderPath = path.join(task.mdDirectory, task.folderName);
>   try {
>     if (fs.readdirSync(folderPath).length === 0) {
>       fs.rmdirSync(folderPath);
>     }
>   } catch { /* ÂøΩÁï• */ }
> ```
> 
> This reads the directory entries and checks if it's empty. But what if `downloadFile` failed (e.g., network error) and the task folder has some files from other successful downloads, but `successCount === 0`? Then the folder won't be deleted. The spec says "ÊóÝ‰ªª‰ΩïÊàêÂõæ" (no images at all) - so if some jobs failed but some images were downloaded, the folder is kept. This is correct. ‚úì
> 
> But actually, if `successCount === 0`, we should still have downloaded 0 images. If the folder is non-empty (has some unrelated files?), it's not deleted. This seems fine.
> 
> Actually wait, could there be image files in the folder from partial downloads? Let me check... `downloadFile` is only called when `result.status === 'succeeded'`. If `successCount === 0`, no downloads happened. So the folder should be empty (or contain only the empty `.` directory entry, which `readdirSync` returns as `.`). Actually, `readdirSync` without `withFileTypes` would return `['.']` or `['.', '..']`... No, `readdirSync` doesn't include `.` and `..` entries. Empty directory returns `[]`. OK, the `.length === 0` check is correct.
> 
> Wait, actually there's a subtle issue in `downloadFile`. If it throws partway through (after creating the file via `writeFileSync` but before...), the file could exist. But `downloadFile` is synchronous after fetch - it reads all data then writes it atomically. If the fetch fails, `writeFileSync` is never called. If the write fails, the error propagates up. The only risk is if `writeFileSync` partially writes data and the system crashes, but that's a system-level issue.
> 
> OK, but there's another issue: `fs.readdirSync(folderPath)` in `finishTask` could fail if the folder was never created. In `createTaskFolder`, `fs.mkdirSync` is called. But if `submitGenerate` all fails before creating any folder... wait, no. The folder is created in `submit()` at line 91, before any jobs are started. So the folder always exists when `finishTask` is called. ‚úì
> 
> But in `failTask` (called when all jobs fail to submit), the same folder check is done. Since the folder was created in `submit()`, `failTask` correctly checks and potentially removes it. ‚úì
> 
> Now let me also check: in `resume()`, `submitting` jobs are marked as `failed`. But this happens before `startPolling`. Then `pollAll` would see all jobs as either failed/succeeded/violation, set `allDone = true`, and call `finishTask`. But `finishTask` checks `successCount === 0` and might try to delete the folder, which still exists. ‚úì This seems correct.
> 
> But wait - when `resume()` marks submitting jobs as failed, does it persist immediately? Looking at:
> ```js
> resume() {
>   const stored = this.context.globalState.get<TaskState[]>(STORAGE_KEY) ?? [];
>   const now = Date.now();
>   for (const task of stored) {
>     task.sessionStartTime = now;
>     for (const job of task.jobs) {
>       if (job.status === 'submitting') {
>         job.status = 'failed';
>         job.error = 'Êèê‰∫§Êú™ÂÆåÊàêÔºàÊâ©Â±ïÂ∑≤ÂÖ≥Èó≠Ôºâ';
>       }
>     }
>     this.tasks.set(task.folderName, task);
>   }
>   this.emitUpdate();
>   if (this.tasks.size > 0) {
>     this.startPolling();
>   }
> }
> ```
> 
> It doesn't call `persist()` after marking jobs as failed! The modified tasks are added to the map, but `persist()` is never called. So the state change (submitting ‚Üí failed) is only in memory, not in globalState. If VS Code crashes again before the next poll, the same tasks would be resumed again with the same submitting jobs.
> 
> This is minor since:
> 1. The `failTask` function will eventually clean up the task
> 2. The `finishTask` function will also clean up when pollAll runs
> 
> But it's a bug that the state change isn't persisted immediately.
> 
> **BUG**: `resume()` doesn't call `persist()` after marking submitting jobs as failed.
> 
> Also, in `resume()`, `emitUpdate()` is called but the `tasks` array is empty at this point (`this._currentTasks` hasn't been set yet in sidebar-provider). Wait, `emitUpdate` calls `this._onUpdate(cards, autoSwitch)` which is the callback set by sidebar-provider. But at resume time, the sidebar might not be visible yet (webview not resolved). So `_view` would be null in sidebar-provider, and the message wouldn't be sent. ‚úì This is fine - the webview will get the task data when it sends `init`.
> 
> But then, when the webview sends `init`, `sendInitData` uses `this._currentTasks` which was set by the last `taskManager.onUpdate` call. So the data is eventually correct. ‚úì
> 
> Now let me look at the `submitJob` function more carefully for the "all jobs fail" case:
> 
> ```js
> private async submitJob(...) {
>   try {
>     const jobId = await submitGenerate(...);
>     job.id = jobId;
>     job.status = 'running';
>     job.transientRetries = 0;
>   } catch (err) {
>     if (isTransientError(err) && job.transientRetries < MAX_RETRY) {
>       job.transientRetries++;
>       this.persist();
>       await this.submitJob(task, job, prompt, refImages, config);
>       return;
>     }
>     job.status = 'failed';
>     job.error = err instanceof Error ? err.message : String(err);
>   }
>   this.persist();
>   this.emitUpdate();
> 
>   // Ê£ÄÊü•ÊòØÂê¶ÂÖ®ÈÉ®Êèê‰∫§Â§±Ë¥•
>   const allDone = task.jobs.every(j => j.status !== 'submitting');
>   if (allDone) {
>     const allFailed = task.jobs.every(j => j.status === 'failed');
>     if (allFailed) {
>       this.failTask(task);
>     }
>   }
> }
> ```
> 
> The `allDone` check happens after every individual job completes. But there's a subtle issue: the first job to complete might see `allDone = true` if all other jobs also completed (or failed) before this one. But since `submitJob` is async and all N jobs are started concurrently with `void`, the order is not deterministic.
> 
> For example:
> - Job 1 starts first, takes 2 seconds, fails
> - Job 2 starts immediately after, takes 1 second, fails
> - When Job 2 completes (1s), its `allDone` check: job1 is still 'submitting' ‚Üí `allDone = false`
> - When Job 1 completes (2s), its `allDone` check: job1='failed', job2='failed' ‚Üí `allDone = true` ‚Üí `failTask` called
> 
> This works correctly because the check is `allDone = true` requires ALL jobs to be non-submitting. Even though `submitJob` is called in a loop with `void`, the async execution means each completion triggers the check independently. ‚úì
> 
> But wait, there's a potential issue: `this.failTask(task)` is called from within `submitJob`, and `submitJob` is called with `void` (fire-and-forget). If `failTask` calls `this.tasks.delete(task.folderName)`, and another concurrent `submitJob` invocation tries to access `task.folderName` afterwards, it would be accessing a stale reference. However, all `submitJob` operations on the task have already completed by this point (the `allDone` check means no more jobs are 'submitting'). ‚úì
> 
> Actually, there's another issue with `failTask` being called from `submitJob`. `failTask` calls `this.persist()`, `this.emitUpdate()`, `this.stopPolling()`. Meanwhile, `submitJob` also calls `this.persist()` and `this.emitUpdate()` after the try-catch. So there could be redundant calls. But this is minor - just inefficiency.
> 
> #### Build Configuration Issues
> 
> **Launch.json outFiles for Extension Tests**: Points to `${workspaceFolder}/out/**/*.js` but the test runner compiles to `out/` (from tsconfig.test.json which extends tsconfig.json's `outDir: "out"`). ‚úì
> 
> **Test runner path**: `extensionTestsPath: path.resolve(__dirname, '../')` - this points to `out/test/../` = `out/`. The test runner would load all `.test.js` files from the `out/` directory. ‚úì
> 
> Let me also check if the `extension.ts` handles `generate` and `previewRequest` through command execution vs message handling. I need to see if there's command execution from the webview...
> 
> No, the frontend sends `type: 'generate'` and `type: 'previewRequest'` messages, and there's no handler for these in `sidebar-provider.ts`. The VS Code commands `image-flow.generateImage` and `image-flow.previewRequest` are registered in `extension.ts` for right-click menu use.
> 
> Looking at this more carefully, the proper approach would have been to either:
> 1. Handle the messages in `sidebar-provider.ts` by calling `taskManager.submit()` directly
> 2. Have the frontend use `vscode.commands.executeCommand` to invoke the registered commands
> 
> The current approach tries to use postMessage but doesn't handle the cases.
> 
> OK, I think I have a comprehensive analysis. Let me compile my report.
> 
> One more thing to check - the `finishTask` vs `failTask` interaction. In `submitJob`, when all jobs fail to submit, `failTask` is called. But `failTask` removes the task from the map. Then what about any other running `submitJob` invocations? When `submitJob` is called from the loop `for (const job of jobs) { void this.submitJob(...); }`, all calls are async. If job1 fails first and calls `failTask`, the task is removed from the map. But the `failTask` checks `allDone = task.jobs.every(j => j.status !== 'submitting')`. At this point, the other jobs might still be 'submitting' (not yet started). So `failTask` would NOT be called until all are done.
> 
> Wait, actually `failTask` is called from inside the `allDone` check in `submitJob`. Let me trace through an example:
> - N=2, both jobs 'submitting'
> - job1's `submitJob` is called: `void this.submitJob(task, job1, ...)`
> - job2's `submitJob` is called: `void this.submitJob(task, job2, ...)`
> - job1's fetch completes (fails): job1.status = 'failed', persist, emitUpdate
>   - `allDone = task.jobs.every(j => j.status !== 'submitting')`
>   - job2 is still 'submitting', so `allDone = false`
>   - `failTask` NOT called
> - job2's fetch completes (fails): job2.status = 'failed', persist, emitUpdate
>   - `allDone = true` (both not submitting)
>   - `allFailed = true` (both failed)  
>   - `failTask(task)` called
> 
> OK, this is correct. But what if `failTask` is called twice? The `this.tasks.delete(task.folderName)` in `failTask` is idempotent. And `persist()` would persist the empty state. `emitUpdate()` would emit an empty task list. And `stopPolling()` would be called.
> 
> But wait, what if both jobs' `submitJob` calls reach the `allDone` check at the same time? Due to JS single-threadedness, they don't truly run simultaneously. The microtask queue means one completes fully (including calling `failTask`) before the other's `.then()` continuation runs. But...
> 
> Actually both are `await`-based. Let me think about the event loop:
> 
> ```js
> for (const job of jobs) {
>   void this.submitJob(task, job, finalPrompt, refImages, config);
> }
> ```
> 
> Each `submitJob` is an async function. The `void` means we're fire-and-forgetting each one. They'll run concurrently. Each one has `await submitGenerate(...)` which does the actual fetch. When one completes, its continuation runs.
> 
> When job1's `submitJob` continuation runs (after the await):
> - job1.status = 'running' or 'failed'
> - persist()
> - emitUpdate()
> - Check allDone: if yes, call failTask
> 
> During this continuation, job2's continuation is waiting in the microtask queue. Since JS is single-threaded, job1's entire continuation runs to completion before job2's. So by the time job2 checks `allDone`, job1 has already completed.
> 
> But what if `failTask` removes the task from the map and clears persistence, and then job2's continuation runs? job2 would try to access `task.jobs` which is still valid (the TaskState object reference is still valid even if the task was removed from the Map). So job2 would set its status, then check `allDone` again, and potentially call `failTask` again.
> 
> The second `failTask` call would:
> 1. Try to delete folder (already deleted or checked in first call) - would fail silently with catch
> 2. `this.tasks.delete(task.folderName)` - already deleted, returns false
> 3. `this.persist()` - persists empty map
> 4. `this.emitUpdate()` - emits empty tasks
> 5. `this.stopPolling()` - called again (idempotent)
> 
> This is a minor inefficiency but not a real bug. All state changes are idempotent.
> 
> #### The `handleMessage` `generate`/`previewRequest` issue:
> 
> Let me double-check. In the spec ¬ß8.5: there's "È¢ÑËßàËØ∑Ê±Ç" button and "ÁîüÊàê" button in the workbench. Looking at Workbench.tsx, these call:
> ```
> vscodeApi.postMessage({ type: 'previewRequest', mdPath: '' })
> vscodeApi.postMessage({ type: 'generate', mdPath: '' })
> ```
> 
> In sidebar-provider.ts handleMessage switch, these types are NOT handled. So clicking these buttons does nothing.
> 
> But there's also the right-click menu commands, which work properly through `extension.ts` command registrations.
> 
> This is clearly a bug - the sidebar buttons don't work. The fix would be to add handler cases for `generate` and `previewRequest` in the switch.
> 
> #### Auto asset folder layer ordering
> 
> Looking at `autoAssetFolders`:
> ```js
> while (current !== workspaceRoot && current !== path.dirname(current)) {
>   // ... check for images ...
>   current = path.dirname(current);
>   if (current === workspaceRoot || current === path.dirname(workspaceRoot)) break;
> }
> return folders.reverse();
> ```
> 
> The function walks from mdDir up to workspaceRoot, collecting folders that have images. Then reverses the array. The spec says "‰ªéÂ∑•‰ΩúÂå∫ÊÝπÁöÑ‰∏ã‰∏ÄÂ±ÇÁõÆÂΩïËµ∑ÔºåÂà∞ MD ÊâÄÂú®ÁõÆÂΩï‰∏∫Ê≠¢ÔºåÊØè‰∏ÄÂ±ÇÂêÑÊàê‰∏Ä‰∏™Â∫ìÔºà‰∏çÂê´Â∑•‰ΩúÂå∫ÊÝπÈÇ£Â±ÇÔºâ". So the ordering should be from workspace child to mdDir.
> 
> But the spec says the library should include "ÊØè‰∏ÄÂ±Ç" (each layer) from the workspace root's next layer TO mdDir's layer. The current implementation walks from mdDir up to workspaceRoot. It skips workspaceRoot itself. Then reverses.
> 
> Let me trace: workspaceRoot=/ws, mdDir=/ws/sub1/sub2
> - current=/ws/sub1/sub2 ‚Üí check, current=/ws/sub1
> - current=/ws/sub1 ‚Üí check, current=/ws (workspaceRoot, no check per break condition)
> - folders = [/ws/sub1/sub2 if has images, /ws/sub1 if has images]
> - folders.reverse() = [/ws/sub1 if has images, /ws/sub1/sub2 if has images]
> 
> This starts from the layer closest to workspace root and goes to mdDir's layer. ‚úì
> 
> But wait, there's a break condition:
> ```js
> if (current === workspaceRoot || current === path.dirname(workspaceRoot)) break;
> ```
> 
> This is inside the while loop. But the while condition already checks `current !== workspaceRoot`. So the first part `current === workspaceRoot` would never trigger inside the loop. The second part `current === path.dirname(workspaceRoot)` is an additional safety check. This seems fine.
> 
> Actually, there might be an issue. After the loop iteration for `/ws/sub1`:
> - current becomes `/ws` (dirname of /ws/sub1)
> - While condition: `/ws !== /ws` ‚Üí false ‚Üí loop exits
> - The `break` inside the loop would never trigger because the while condition catches it first
> 
> So the `break` inside the loop after `current = path.dirname(current)` is actually dead code for the workspaceRoot case but protects against an unlikely scenario where dirname equals itself (root). This is fine.
>

> case 'previewRequest': {

The `extension.ts` command handler checks `!uri.fsPath.endsWith('.md')` - since `_effectiveMd` is always a .md file, this would pass. ‚úì

> Actually wait, let me also check for one more potential issue: the `Workbench.tsx` sends `mdPath: ''` for generate/preview. But the `FrontendMessage` type has `mdPath: string` which allows empty string. If we added handler support, we'd need to use `_effectiveMd` from the sidebar provider.

> Let me also check if there's a missing `init` reply that should include asset folders:
> 
> In `sidebar.tsx`, on init:
> ```ts
> case 'initData':
>   setConfig(msg.data.config);
>   setEffectiveMd(msg.data.effectiveMd);
>   setTasks(msg.data.tasks);
>   break;
> ```
> 
> The `msg.data.assetFolders` is received but not used. It's `assetFolders: []` from `sendInitData` anyway. The actual asset folders are sent later via `sendAssetUpdate`. This is fine - the two-step init (initData then assetUpdate) is intentional.
> 
> Actually wait, looking at `sidebar.tsx` again:
> ```ts
> const [assetFolders, setAssetFolders] = useState<AssetFolderInfo[]>([]);
> ```
> And the `initData` handler doesn't set `assetFolders`. But the `assetUpdate` handler does:
> ```ts
> case 'assetUpdate':
>   setAssetFolders(msg.folders);
>   break;
> ```
> 
> The `sendInitData` calls `this.sendAssetUpdate(webview)` which sends an `assetUpdate` message. So asset folders arrive via that path. The `assetFolders` field in `InitData` is never used by the frontend. It's essentially dead weight in the protocol. MINOR.
> 
> OK, I have a very thorough understanding now. Let me compile my findings.
> 
> Let me also check whether there's an issue with the `setConfig` handler in sidebar-provider. Looking at:
> 
> ```ts
> case 'setConfig': {
>   await writeConfigField(this.context, msg.key, msg.value);
>   const config = await readConfig(this.context);
>   this.postMessage(webview, { type: 'configUpdate', config });
>   break;
> }
> ```
> 
> This calls `writeConfigField` then `readConfig`. For `apiKey`, there's also a `setApiKey` message handler:
> ```ts
> case 'setApiKey': {
>   await this.context.secrets.store('imageFlow.apiKey', msg.key);
>   break;
> }
> ```
> 
> But in `Settings.tsx`:
> ```tsx
> onChange={e => {
>   const key = e.target.value;
>   vscodeApi.postMessage({ type: 'setApiKey', key });
>   onConfigChange('apiKey', key);
> }}
> ```
> 
> And `onConfigChange` is `sendConfig` which posts `{ type: 'setConfig', key, value }`. So when the API key changes:
> 1. `setApiKey` is sent ‚Üí stores in secrets
> 2. `setConfig` with `key: 'apiKey'` is sent ‚Üí calls `writeConfigField` with key='apiKey', which also stores in secrets
> 
> So the API key is stored twice. This is redundant but not harmful. **MINOR issue.**
> 
> Also, after `writeConfigField('apiKey', key)` is called (in `setConfig` handler), the handler then calls `readConfig` and sends `configUpdate` back. This `configUpdate` message includes the new apiKey value (which will be masked by the frontend). Wait, looking at `sendInitData`, the apiKey is masked:
> ```ts
> config: { ...config, apiKey: config.apiKey ? '‚Ä¢‚Ä¢‚Ä¢‚Ä¢‚Ä¢‚Ä¢‚Ä¢‚Ä¢' : '' },
> ```
> But in `configUpdate`, the config is sent as-is:
> ```ts
> this.postMessage(webview, { type: 'configUpdate', config });
> ```
> 
> So the `configUpdate` message would include the plaintext apiKey in the backend ‚Üí frontend message. Let me check how the frontend uses this...
> 
> In `sidebar.tsx`:
> ```ts
> case 'configUpdate':
>   setConfig(prev => prev ? { ...prev, ...msg.config } : null);
>   break;
> ```
> 
> And in `Settings.tsx`:
> ```tsx
> <input type="password" value={config.apiKey} ... />
> ```
> 
> So the configUpdate would send the actual apiKey to the frontend. The frontend then uses it as the input value. But the `setApiKey` message is also sent, and separately the `setConfig` message is sent. The `setConfig` handler in sidebar-provider calls `readConfig` which reads from secrets and sends the plaintext apiKey back to the frontend.
> 
> This is a **security concern** - the apiKey is sent over postMessage to the webview. While webview postMessage is within the same VS Code process (not over the network), the apiKey is exposed in the webview's JavaScript memory. This is considered an acceptable risk for VS Code extensions (it's how most extensions handle it).
> 
> But there's a subtle issue: when the `setConfig` handler calls `readConfig` and sends `configUpdate`, the apiKey comes back as plaintext. This is then used to populate the password input. Meanwhile, the `setApiKey` handler has already stored the key. This is fine.
> 
> Actually, the bigger issue is that `setConfig` with `key='apiKey'` and `setApiKey` both try to store the API key, and the `setConfig` path then sends it back in plaintext. This is redundant.
>

> Actually, one more thing about the `downloadFile` - it's called from `pollAll` inside the polling loop. If `downloadFile` fails (network error, write error), the error is caught by the try-catch in `pollAll`:
> 
> ```ts
> try {
>   const result = await queryResult(config.baseUrl, config.apiKey, job.id);
>   job.progress = result.progress ?? job.progress;
>   if (result.status === 'succeeded' && result.results) {
>     // ...
>     await downloadFile(r.url, dest);  // Might throw!
>     task.downloadedImages.push(filename);
>   }
> } catch (err) {
>   if (isTransientError(err)) {
>     allDone = false;  // Keep trying
>   } else {
>     job.status = 'failed';  // Mark as failed
>     job.error = err instanceof Error ? err.message : String(err);
>   }
> }
> ```
> 
> If `downloadFile` throws, it's caught here. If it's a transient error (like network failure during download), the job stays 'running' and retries next poll. But `downloadFile` doesn't use `ApiError` - it throws a generic `Error`. So `isTransientError` would check:
> - `err instanceof ApiError` ‚Üí No
> - `err.name === 'AbortError'` ‚Üí No (no timeout on download)
> - `err instanceof TypeError && (err.message.includes('fetch') || err.message.includes('abort'))` ‚Üí Maybe if fetch fails
> 
> For most download failures (404, server error, etc.), `isTransientError` returns false, so the job would be marked as failed and the image lost. But the image URL might be valid on retry... This is acceptable behavior - a failed download means the image can't be recovered.
> 
> The real issue is: `downloadFile` has NO timeout. If the download takes too long (e.g., image server is slow), it could block the pollAll reentrant lock for an arbitrarily long time. But since `downloadFile` is inside the try block, if it takes too long, the whole poll is delayed, and the reentrant lock prevents any other polls from running. During this time, no other jobs are polled. This is a **CRITICAL BUG** for the case of a slow image server.
> 
> OK, now I have all the information. Let me compile the final report.
> 
> Let me check one more thing about whether extension.ts properly passes context.subscriptions for disposal. In spec ¬ß3: "ÊâÄÊúâÂèØÈáäÊîæÂØπË±°ÔºàÂëΩ‰ª§„ÄÅÁõëÂê¨Âô®„ÄÅ‰ªªÂä°ÁÆ°ÁêÜÂô®ÔºâÂøÖÈ°ª push Ëøõ `context.subscriptions`" - Looking at extension.ts:
> - `registerWebviewViewProvider` ‚Üí pushed ‚úì
> - `registerCommand('image-flow.generateImage')` ‚Üí pushed ‚úì
> - `registerCommand('image-flow.previewRequest')` ‚Üí pushed ‚úì
> - `taskManager` ‚Üí pushed ‚úì
> 
> But what about `sidebarProvider`? It has a `dispose()` method but is not in context.subscriptions. The `sidebarProvider` is created as a local variable but not disposed on deactivation. Its `_editorListener` would not be cleaned up.
> 
> Actually, looking at it, `sidebarProvider` is used as the provider for `registerWebviewViewProvider`. VS Code holds a reference to it. On deactivation, things registered in `context.subscriptions` are disposed. But `sidebarProvider` isn't in subscriptions. However, VS Code also cleans up the webview view provider when the extension deactivates. The question is whether `sidebarProvider.dispose()` is ever called.
> 
> Looking at the code, `deactivate()` is empty (line 88). So when the extension deactivates:
> 1. context.subscriptions are disposed (by VS Code runtime)
>    - This disposes the registered commands and the webview view provider registration
>    - It also disposes `taskManager` (which stops the polling timer)
> 2. `sidebarProvider.dispose()` is NEVER called
>    - The `_editorListener` is NOT disposed
> 
> **BUG**: `sidebarProvider` is not added to `context.subscriptions`, so its `_editorListener` leaks on deactivation.
> 
> The fix would be to add `context.subscriptions.push(sidebarProvider)` in `extension.ts`.
> 
> Wait, actually the `sideBarProvider` has a `dispose()` method that only disposes `_editorListener`. If we add it to `context.subscriptions`, VS Code would call `dispose()` on deactivation. Let me confirm...
> 
> Looking at VS Code API: `context.subscriptions.push({ dispose })` - any object with a `dispose()` method can be pushed. The runtime calls dispose on all subscriptions when the extension deactivates. Since `SidebarProvider` has a `dispose()` method, it can be added. But it's not currently.
> 
> OK, this is confirmed as a bug.
> 
> One more edge case: what if the user opens the sidebar webview but VS Code calls `resolveWebviewView` twice (e.g., the panel is hidden and reshown)? Each call creates a new `_editorListener` without disposing the old one. Let me verify:
> 
> ```ts
> resolveWebviewView(webviewView: vscode.WebviewView) {
>   this._view = webviewView;
>   // ...
>   this._editorListener = vscode.window.onDidChangeActiveTextEditor(editor => {
>     // ...
>   });
> }
> ```
> 
> If called twice:
> 1. `this._editorListener` from the first call is lost (no `.dispose()` called)
> 2. `this._view` is overwritten with the new webview
> 3. A new listener is registered
> 
> The old listener leaks. And it references the old `_view` via `this._view!` which is now pointing to the new webview. So even the old listener would try to post to the new webview, which is probably wrong but not harmful if the webview handles it.
> 
> Actually, the listener uses `this._view!` which is the current `_view` reference (now pointing to the new webview). So both listeners would send messages to the same (new) webview. This means the effectiveMd change would be notified twice. Not a crash but redundant messages.
> 
> **FIX**: In `resolveWebviewView`, the existing `_editorListener` should be disposed before creating a new one:
> ```ts
> this._editorListener?.dispose();
> this._editorListener = vscode.window.onDidChangeActiveTextEditor(editor => { ... });
> ```
> 
> Now let me also check: are there any `thenable` promises that are not handled? In `sidebar-provider.ts`:
> - `this.context.secrets.store(...)`: returns Thenable but not awaited in `setApiKey` handler. Actually, it IS awaited: `await this.context.secrets.store('imageFlow.apiKey', msg.key)`. ‚úì
> - `editor.edit(...)`: returns Thenable, awaited with `await`. ‚úì
>

This means the disabled/generating state for the button cannot work without additional protocol messages.

> 2. `taskManager.submit()` is called
> 3. `taskManager.emitUpdate(true)` is called (with autoSwitch=true)
> 4. sidebar-provider receives the update
> 5. If webview is visible, `taskUpdate` with `autoSwitch=true` is sent

> But wait - the right-click menu likely from the editor/explorer doesn't involve the sidebar. After submission, the side bar should auto-switch to tasks. Let me trace this:
> 
> 1. Right-click ‚Üí `extension.ts` command handler ‚Üí `taskManager.submit(mdUri)`
> 2. Inside submit: `this.emitUpdate(true)` 
> 3. `emitUpdate` calls `this._onUpdate(cards, autoSwitch)`
> 4. The callback set by sidebar-provider: `taskManager.onUpdate = (tasks, autoSwitch) => { ... this.postMessage(... taskUpdate ... autoSwitch) }`
> 5. `this._view` must be non-null for the message to be sent
> 
> If the sidebar is not visible when the right-click happens, `this._view` is null, and the autoSwitch message is never sent. When the user later opens the sidebar, the webview sends `init`, which just shows the current data. The autoSwitch won't happen.

> So the auto-switch to tasks tab only works if the sidebar is already open when the generation starts. This is a minor UX issue.

> Actually, looking at it more carefully: when the user right-clicks, the spec says "Âè≥ÈîÆËèúÂçïËß¶ÂèëÁîüÊàêÊó∂ÔºåÁõÆÊÝá MD ÂêåÊó∂Êàê‰∏∫ÁîüÊïà MD Âπ∂ËÅöÁÑ¶‰æßÊÝè" (¬ß8.2). So the right-click should also focus/activate the sidebar! This isn't implemented.

> Current Workbench doesn't disable the button for empty mdFileName. **Minor spec gap.**
> 
> Actually, looking at the spec requirement again: "ÁîüÊàêË°åÔºöÊòæÁ§∫ÂΩìÂâçÁîüÊïà MD Êñá‰ª∂ÂêçÔºàÊóÝÂàô„ÄåÊú™ÊâìÂºÄ Markdown Êñá‰ª∂„ÄçÔºâ+„ÄåÈ¢ÑËßàËØ∑Ê±Ç„ÄçÊåâÈíÆ +„ÄåÁîüÊàê„ÄçÊåâÈíÆÔºàÊèê‰∫§ÊúüÈó¥Á¶ÅÁî®Âπ∂ÊòæÁ§∫„ÄåÁîüÊàê‰∏≠‚Ä¶„ÄçÔºâ"
> 
> The key parts:
> 1. Show MD filename ‚úì (shown as `mdFileName`)
> 2. If no MD, show "Êú™ÊâìÂºÄ Markdown Êñá‰ª∂" ‚úì (sidebar.tsx line 62)
> 3. Generate button disabled during submission ‚ùå (not implemented)
> 4. Generate button shows "ÁîüÊàê‰∏≠‚Ä¶" during submission ‚ùå (not implemented)
> 
> OK, I have a complete analysis. Let me write the report now.
> 
> Actually, let me also check one more thing: the `handleInsertReference` in sidebar-provider. The spec says:
> "‰ªÖÂΩìÂΩìÂâçÊ¥ªÂä®ÁºñËæëÂô®Ê≠£ÊòØÁîüÊïà MD Êó∂ÊâçÊèíÂÖ•ÔºåÂê¶ÂàôË≠¶ÂëäÂπ∂ÂøΩÁï•ÔºàÈò≤ÊèíÈîôÊñá‰ª∂Ôºâ"
> 
> The current check is:
> ```ts
> const docUri = editor.document.uri;
> if (!docUri.fsPath.endsWith('.md')) return;
> ```
> 
> This should be:
> ```ts
> if (docUri.fsPath !== this._effectiveMd) {
>   vscode.window.showWarningMessage('ËØ∑Âú®ÁîüÊïà Markdown Êñá‰ª∂‰∏≠ÊèíÂÖ•ÂºïÁî®');
>   return;
> }
> ```
> 
> **IMPORTANT spec gap**: Not checking against `_effectiveMd`.
> 
> Now about the `imageRelativeRef` function: it uses `path.posix.relative(mdDir, imagePath)`. But `mdDir` and `imagePath` are both local filesystem paths (with backslashes on Windows). `path.posix.relative` treats them as POSIX paths, which means on Windows, it would try to compute the relative path treating backslashes as regular characters. The paths are normalized with `.replace(/\\/g, '/')` afterward, but the `path.posix.relative` call itself might not work correctly on Windows.
> 
> For example, on Windows:
> - mdDir = `C:\Users\me\project`
> - imagePath = `C:\Users\me\project\img.png`
> - `path.posix.relative(mdDir, imagePath)` would compute the relative path between `C:\Users\me\project` and `C:\Users\me\project\img.png`
> - Using posix path semantics, both paths have no common prefix (due to `C:` vs `C:` looking like different paths)
> - Result would be something like `../../C:/Users/me/project/img.png`
> 
> This is a **cross-platform bug** on Windows. The fix would be to use `path.relative` and then convert separators.
> 
> OK now I'm truly done analyzing. Let me write the final report.
</details>

> 
> ## Image-Flow Implementation Review: Spec Gap Analysis & Bug Hunting
> 
> I have read every source file in `src/` and `media/components/`, plus the spec, config, and build files. Below are findings organized by priority.
> 
> ---
> 
> ### CRITICAL
> 
> #### C1. Sidebar "Generate" and "Preview Request" buttons do nothing
> 
> The frontend `Workbench.tsx` posts messages `{ type: 'generate', mdPath: '' }` and `{ type: 'previewRequest', mdPath: '' }`, but the backend `handleMessage` switch in `sidebar-provider.ts` has no cases for these types -- they fall through to `default: break`. The right-click commands registered in `extension.ts` work, but the main sidebar buttons are dead.
> 
> **SPEC**: SS8.3 (generate row with buttons is a core UI element)
> **SOURCE**: `media/components/Workbench.tsx:55-56` sends the messages; `src/sidebar-provider.ts:100-137` switch does not handle `'generate'` or `'previewRequest'`.
> 
> ---
> 
> #### C2. Task and history thumbnails cannot display in the webview
> 
> `TaskCardInfo.downloadedImages` for active tasks contains bare filenames (e.g., `"mydoc-1.png"`) from `task-manager.ts:226`, and for history tasks contains raw absolute filesystem paths from `sidebar-provider.ts:245`. Neither is converted via `webview.asWebviewUri()`, so `<img src={img}>` in `TaskCard.tsx:44` will fail -- VS Code webviews cannot load arbitrary filesystem paths.
> 
> Additionally, `localResourceRoots` in `sidebar-provider.ts:60-71` only contains the `media/` directory and workspace roots. It does NOT include manually-added asset library folders that lie outside the workspace, and it is never updated after initial creation (no mechanism to call `webview.options = ...` again).
> 
> **SPEC**: SS8.1 ("Êú¨Âú∞ÂõæÁâáÂøÖÈ°ªÁªè `asWebviewUri` ËΩ¨Êç¢ÂêéÊâçËÉΩÂú® webview ÊòæÁ§∫") and SS8.1 ("`localResourceRoots` ÂåÖÂê´Êâ©Â±ï media ÁõÆÂΩï„ÄÅÂ∑•‰ΩúÂå∫ÁõÆÂΩï‰∏éÂÖ®ÈÉ®Á¥ÝÊùêÂ∫ìÁõÆÂΩïÔºåÁ¥ÝÊùêÂ∫ìÂ¢ûÂàÝÂêéÈúÄÈáçËÆæ")
> **SOURCE**: `src/sidebar-provider.ts:245` (history paths raw), `src/task-manager.ts:226+347` (active task filenames raw), `src/sidebar-provider.ts:60-71` (missing external folders, no update path), `media/components/TaskCard.tsx:44` (uses raw src)
> 
> ---
> 
> #### C3. `downloadFile` has no timeout, can permanently block the poll loop
> 
> The helper `downloadFile` at `task-manager.ts:377-382` calls `fetch(url)` without any timeout mechanism. If the image server hangs, this blocks inside `pollAll` (line 225), and because of the reentrant lock (line 191), no subsequent poll cycles can run. The spec requires "ÊâÄÊúâÁΩëÁªúËØ∑Ê±ÇÂ∏¶Ë∂ÖÊó∂Ôºà30s ÈáèÁ∫ßÔºâ" at SS6.
> 
> **SPEC**: SS6 (timeout on all network requests), SS7.2 (polling reentrant lock)
> **SOURCE**: `src/task-manager.ts:377-382` (no timeout), `src/task-manager.ts:190-258` (pollAll reentrant lock)
> 
> ---
> 
> #### C4. SidebarProvider `_editorListener` leaks on every webview re-create
> 
> `resolveWebviewView` at `sidebar-provider.ts:50` creates a new `this._editorListener` via `vscode.window.onDidChangeActiveTextEditor(...)` without disposing the previous listener. VS Code can call `resolveWebviewView` multiple times (webview hidden/reshown, panel reopened), causing a leak of editor-change listeners. Furthermore, `SidebarProvider` is never added to `context.subscriptions`, so its `dispose()` method (which only cleans `_editorListener`) is never called on deactivation.
> 
> **SPEC**: SS3 ("ÊâÄÊúâÂèØÈáäÊîæÂØπË±°...ÂøÖÈ°ª push Ëøõ `context.subscriptions`")
> **SOURCE**: `src/sidebar-provider.ts:50-57` (no old listener disposal), `src/extension.ts:17` (provider not pushed to subscriptions), `src/sidebar-provider.ts:276-278` (dispose only cleans _editorListener)
> 
> ---
> 
> ### IMPORTANT
> 
> #### I1. Generate button never disabled or labeled "ÁîüÊàê‰∏≠..." during submission
> 
> The spec at SS8.3 requires the generate button to be disabled and show "ÁîüÊàê‰∏≠..." while a submission is in progress. The current `Workbench.tsx:56` renders a static `<button>ÁîüÊàê</button>` with no disabled state. The frontend has no `isSubmitting` state, and there is no backend message to toggle it.
> 
> **SPEC**: SS8.3 ("Êèê‰∫§ÊúüÈó¥Á¶ÅÁî®Âπ∂ÊòæÁ§∫„ÄåÁîüÊàê‰∏≠‚Ä¶„Äç")
> **SOURCE**: `media/components/Workbench.tsx:56`
> 
> #### I2. Task/history cards have no collapse/expand behavior
> 
> The spec at SS8.4 specifies: active cards should be "ÈªòËÆ§Â±ïÂºÄÔºåÂèØÊäòÂèÝ" and history cards "ÈªòËÆ§Êî∂Ëµ∑". The current `TaskCard.tsx` renders all content unconditionally -- no collapsible mechanism. The `@radix-ui/react-collapsible` dependency exists in `package.json` but is never imported.

> **SPEC**: SS8.4

> 
> #### I3. Thumbnail click does not open the original image in VS Code
> 
> The spec at SS8.4 requires "Áº©Áï•ÂõæÁÇπÂáªÁî® VS Code ÊâìÂºÄÂéüÂõæ". The `<img>` elements in `TaskCard.tsx:44` have no `onClick` handler.

> 
> #### I4. `tasksCols` config value is never applied to the task grid
> 
> `Tasks.tsx` receives the `cols` prop (named `_cols` with underscore to suppress the unused-variable warning) but never applies it. The task image grid uses a CSS `grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fill, minmax(40px, 1fr))` from `sidebar.css:56` instead of the config-driven value.
> 
> **SPEC**: SS8.4 ("ÊØèË°åÂºÝÊï∞Áî± `tasksCols` ÊéßÂà∂"), SS4.1 (`tasksCols` config)
> **SOURCE**: `media/components/Tasks.tsx:11` (unused `_cols`), `media/sidebar.css:56` (hardcoded CSS)
> 
> #### I5. Right-click "Generate" does not set effective MD or focus the sidebar
> 
> The spec at SS8.2 requires: "Âè≥ÈîÆËèúÂçïËß¶ÂèëÁîüÊàêÊó∂ÔºåÁõÆÊÝá MD ÂêåÊó∂Êàê‰∏∫ÁîüÊïà MD Âπ∂ËÅöÁÑ¶‰æßÊÝè." The command handler in `extension.ts:27-42` only calls `taskManager.submit(mdUri)` and does not call `sidebarProvider.setEffectiveMd()` or focus the sidebar view. Similarly, the `image-flow.previewRequest` command handler (line 45) calls none of these.
> 
> **SPEC**: SS8.2
> **SOURCE**: `src/extension.ts:27-83` (no sidebar focus or effective-md set)
> 
> #### I6. Insert reference does not verify the active editor is `_effectiveMd`
> 
> The spec at SS10.3 requires: "‰ªÖÂΩìÂΩìÂâçÊ¥ªÂä®ÁºñËæëÂô®Ê≠£ÊòØÁîüÊïà MD Êó∂ÊâçÊèíÂÖ•ÔºåÂê¶ÂàôË≠¶ÂëäÂπ∂ÂøΩÁï•ÔºàÈò≤ÊèíÈîôÊñá‰ª∂Ôºâ." The current `handleInsertReference` at `sidebar-provider.ts:139-152` only checks that the document extension is `.md`, not that it matches `this._effectiveMd`. No warning is shown on mismatch.
> 
> **SPEC**: SS10.3
> **SOURCE**: `src/sidebar-provider.ts:139-148`
> 
> #### I7. Resume does not persist state changes for submitting jobs
> 
> When `resume()` marks stale 'submitting' jobs as 'failed' (lines 52-56 of `task-manager.ts`), it does not call `this.persist()`. If the extension is deactivated again before the next poll, the same jobs will be resumed again in 'submitting' state.
> 
> **SPEC**: SS7.4 ("Áä∂ÊÄÅÂèòÊõ¥ÈöèÊó∂ËêΩÁõò")
> **SOURCE**: `src/task-manager.ts:47-64` (no `persist()` after marking submitting jobs)
> 
> #### I8. Sidebar on-did-change listener may race with init
> 
> When the webview first loads, it immediately sends `'init'`. The `sendInitData` handler returns `effectiveMd: this._effectiveMd` which is `''` because the `onDidChangeActiveTextEditor` listener registered in `resolveWebviewView` fires asynchronously after the init. This causes a brief flash of "Êú™ÊâìÂºÄ Markdown Êñá‰ª∂" before the correction arrives. The listener should be interrogated synchronously during `resolveWebviewView` to capture the current active editor.
> 
> **SPEC**: SS8.2 (ÁîüÊïà MD Ë∑üÈöè)
> **SOURCE**: `src/sidebar-provider.ts:35-57` (listener async, init runs before it fires)
> 
> #### I9. `compile` script does not pre-execute `check-types` and `lint`
> 
> The spec at SS2 requires "`compile` / `package` ËÑöÊú¨ÂâçÁΩÆÊâßË°å `check-types` ‰∏é `lint`". The current `compile` script at `package.json:109` is just `node esbuild.config.mjs` with no type-check or lint step. Only the `package` script chains them.
> 
> **SPEC**: SS2
> **SOURCE**: `package.json:109`
> 
> #### I10. `gpt-image-2-vip` pixel conversion has no unit test
> 
> The spec at SS12 explicitly requires tests for "gpt-image-2-vip ÂÉèÁ¥ÝÊç¢ÁÆó." The function `vipPixelValue` in `image-utils.ts:43-48` has no corresponding test file coverage -- the existing tests cover markdown parsing, progress, transient errors, preview, and asset library, but not pixel conversion.
> 
> **SPEC**: SS12
> **SOURCE**: `src/image-utils.ts:43-48` (untested), no test file covers it
> 
> ---
> 
> ### MINOR
> 
> #### M1. Redundant API key storage through two message paths
> 
> `Settings.tsx` sends both a `'setApiKey'` message (processed at `sidebar-provider.ts:112-114`, stores in secrets) and a `'setConfig'` message with `key: 'apiKey'` (processed at `sidebar-provider.ts:107-110`, also calls `writeConfigField` which stores in secrets again). The API key is written to secrets twice per keystroke.
> 
> **SOURCE**: `media/components/Settings.tsx:21-25`, `src/sidebar-provider.ts:107-115`
> 
> #### M2. `extToMime` falls back to `image/png` unconditionally
> 
> For unknown extensions, `extToMime` returns `'image/png'` instead of rejecting. Combined with the markdown parser not validating that referenced files are actual images, this could send non-image data with an incorrect MIME type to the API.
> 
> **SOURCE**: `src/image-utils.ts:30-32`, `src/markdown-parser.ts:38`
> 
> #### M3. Results array items not individually validated
> 
> `queryResult` at `api.ts:166` casts `d.results as { url: string }[]` without verifying each item's shape. If the API returns `results: [{ url: null }]` or `results: [{ notUrl: true }]`, the invalid data flows through silently.
> 
> **SOURCE**: `src/api.ts:166`
> 
> #### M4. `Workbench.tsx` sends empty `mdPath: ''` for generate/preview
> 
> The frontend sends `mdPath: ''` (empty string) for generate and preview requests. Even with a proper backend handler, the handler would need to read `this._effectiveMd` from `SidebarProvider` rather than from the message payload. The `FrontendMessage` type should either omit `mdPath` for these messages or the frontend should send the actual path.
> 
> **SOURCE**: `media/components/Workbench.tsx:55-56`, `src/shared.ts:4-5`
> 
> #### M5. `autoAssetFolders` has a redundant break condition
> 
> The inner-loop break at `asset-library.ts:61` (`current === path.dirname(workspaceRoot)`) is dead code for normal paths -- the while condition `current !== workspaceRoot` catches the workspace root boundary first. The condition only matters if `path.dirname()` of the workspace root somehow equals a different directory on edge-case filesystems.
> 
> **SOURCE**: `src/asset-library.ts:61`
> 
> #### M6. `initData.assetFolders` field is never consumed by the frontend
> 
> `sendInitData` populates `data.assetFolders` with an empty array, and the `'initData'` handler in `sidebar.tsx:25-28` ignores `msg.data.assetFolders`. Asset folders arrive separately via `'assetUpdate'`. The field in `InitData` is dead protocol weight.
> 
> **SOURCE**: `src/shared.ts:19`, `src/sidebar-provider.ts:199`, `media/sidebar.tsx:25-28`
> 
> #### M7. API key sent as plaintext in `configUpdate` message
> 
> When the settings page stores a new API key, the `'setConfig'` handler at `sidebar-provider.ts:107-110` calls `readConfig` and sends the plaintext key back to the webview via `configUpdate`. While the `'initData'` handler masks the key, `configUpdate` does not. The key ends up in JavaScript memory in the webview (acceptable-risk pattern for VS Code, but inconsistent with init masking).
> 
> **SOURCE**: `src/sidebar-provider.ts:107-110` vs `src/sidebar-provider.ts:196`
> 
> #### M8. `imageRelativeRef` may produce wrong results on Windows
> 
> The function at `asset-library.ts:113` uses `path.posix.relative(mdDir, imagePath)` which treats both arguments as POSIX paths. On Windows, `C:\Users\...\img.png` and `C:\Users\...\` have no common prefix in POSIX semantics, producing an incorrect relative path. The subsequent `.replace(/\\/g, '/')` cannot fix the wrong base calculation.
> 
> **SOURCE**: `src/asset-library.ts:113`
>

<summary><code>/home/particle/2026-1/harness-human-research/image-flow/runs/eval-manual-superpowers-260611093413/src/extension.ts</code></summary>

```typescript
import * as vscode from 'vscode';
import * as path from 'path';
import * as fs from 'fs';
import { parseMarkdown } from './markdown-parser';
import { buildRequestBody } from './api';
import { seedModelInjections, readConfig } from './config';
import { TaskManager } from './task-manager';
import { SidebarProvider } from './sidebar-provider';
import { assemblePrompt } from './prompt-injection';
import { showPreview } from './preview';

export function activate(context: vscode.ExtensionContext) {
  // ÂÖàÁßçÂÖ•ÁßçÂ≠êÔºàÂøÖÈ°ªÂú®Ê≥®ÂÜå‰æßÊÝè‰πãÂâçÔºâ
  void seedModelInjections(context);

  const taskManager = new TaskManager(context);
  const sidebarProvider = new SidebarProvider(context, taskManager);

  context.subscriptions.push(
    vscode.window.registerWebviewViewProvider('image-flow.sidebar', sidebarProvider)
  );

  // Áª≠ÊãâÊú™ÂÆåÊàê‰ªªÂä°
  taskManager.resume();

  context.subscriptions.push(
    vscode.commands.registerCommand('image-flow.generateImage', async (uri?: vscode.Uri) => {
      const mdUri = uri ?? vscode.window.activeTextEditor?.document.uri;
      if (!mdUri || !mdUri.fsPath.endsWith('.md')) {
        vscode.window.showErrorMessage('ËØ∑Âè≥ÈîÆÁÇπÂáª Markdown Êñá‰ª∂ÊàñÊâìÂºÄ .md Êñá‰ª∂ÂêéÊâßË°å');
        return;
      }

      try {
        await taskManager.submit(mdUri);
      } catch (err) {
        vscode.window.showErrorMessage(
          err instanceof Error ? err.message : String(err)
        );
      }
    })
  );

  context.subscriptions.push(
    vscode.commands.registerCommand('image-flow.previewRequest', async (uri?: vscode.Uri) => {
      const mdUri = uri ?? vscode.window.activeTextEditor?.document.uri;
      if (!mdUri || !mdUri.fsPath.endsWith('.md')) {
        vscode.window.showErrorMessage('ËØ∑Âè≥ÈîÆÁÇπÂáª Markdown Êñá‰ª∂ÊàñÊâìÂºÄ .md Êñá‰ª∂ÂêéÊâßË°å');
        return;
      }

      const config = await readConfig(context);

      const raw = fs.readFileSync(mdUri.fsPath, 'utf-8');
      if (raw.trim() === '') {
        vscode.window.showErrorMessage('ÂÜÖÂÆπ‰∏∫Á©∫');
        return;
      }

      const mdDir = path.dirname(mdUri.fsPath);
      const { body, images, errors: parseErrors } = parseMarkdown(raw, mdDir);
      if (parseErrors.length > 0) {
        vscode.window.showErrorMessage(`ÂèÇËÄÉÂõæËØªÂèñÂ§±Ë¥•:\n${parseErrors.join('\n')}`);
        return;
      }

      const finalPrompt = assemblePrompt(config.model, config.modelInjections, body, mdDir);

      const requestBody = buildRequestBody({
        baseUrl: config.baseUrl,
        apiKey: config.apiKey,
        model: config.model,
        prompt: finalPrompt,
        images: images.map(i => i.dataUri),
        aspectRatio: config.aspectRatio,
        imageSize: config.imageSize,
      });

      const imageDataUris = images.map(i => i.dataUri);

      await showPreview(finalPrompt, config.baseUrl, requestBody, imageDataUris);
    })
  );

  context.subscriptions.push(taskManager);
}

export function deactivate() {}

```
</details>

> Wait, actually that's not quite right. The spec says: "The [[MapData]] List is never exposed directly to user code." And "The elements of a Map are ordered based on insertion." The iterator is created from the Map's entries at the time `values()` is called, and it iterates based on the current state. > Actually, looking at the ECMAScript 2025 spec for `%MapIteratorPrototype%.next()`:
> - It gets the current [[MapData]] and navigates to the next entry that hasn't been visited

> 4. Looking at the security/error handling:
> - `downloadFile`: uses `fetch(url)` directly, no timeout. If the server responds slowly, this blocks the polling loop. The spec says query polling has 30s timeout, but download doesn't mention a timeout. However, blocking the download could cause the re-entry lock to stay set.
> 
> Actually, the polling lock is per round, not per download. Downloads happen inside `pollAll` which has the re-entry lock. If a download hangs, the entire pollAll round hangs, but `polling` is set to true and the next interval tick will just return early. So subsequent polls are blocked. This is a potential issue but not critical.

> 1. LINE 56-59: `this._editorListener` is registered in `resolveWebviewView`, but `resolveWebviewView` can be called multiple times (VS Code may re-create the webview). Each call would create a new listener without disposing the old one. This would accumulate listeners.

> ```typescript
> this._editorListener?.dispose();
> this._editorListener = vscode.window.onDidChangeActiveTextEditor(...)

> 1. `imageRelativeRef` uses `path.posix.relative(mdDir, imagePath)`. On Windows, `path.posix.relative` uses POSIX separators. But `mdDir` and `imagePath` on Windows would have backslash separators. This could produce incorrect relative paths on Windows. Actually, the function then does `rel = rel.replace(/\\/g, '/')` which handles the conversion. But `path.posix.relative` on Windows paths with backslashes might produce unexpected results because `path.posix` doesn't understand backslashes.
> 
> Actually wait, on Windows, file paths from fs.readFileSync and vs.Uri.fsPath use backslashes. But `path.posix.relative` treats paths as POSIX ‚Äî it doesn't know about backslashes as separators. So it would treat the entire Windows path as a single filename, producing incorrect results.
> 
> However, the line before the relative call does: `path.isAbsolute(rel)` check. On Windows, this would fail because the result from `path.posix.relative` with backslash paths would be incorrect.
> 
> This is a Windows compatibility BUG. The fix would be to use `path.relative` (the OS-native version) and then convert backslashes to forward slashes. Actually wait, looking at the code again:
> 
> ```
> let rel: string;
> try {
>   rel = path.posix.relative(mdDir, imagePath);
> } catch {
>   return null;
> }
> ```
> 
> On Windows, `mdDir` would be like `C:\Users\...` and `imagePath` would be like `C:\Users\...\img.png`. `path.posix.relative` would not understand these paths correctly because they use backslash separators. It would either fail or produce incorrect results.
> 
> The fix: Use `path.relative` (native) first, then convert the result to POSIX style.
>

> 1. LINE 24-27: The `imageTitle` function extracts just the filename without extension from a path. It uses `path.basename` which correctly handles both `/` and `\`. OK.

> 2. The regex `IMAGE_RE = /!\[([^\]]*)\]\((<[^>]+>|[^)\s]+)\)/g` ‚Äî this handles `![alt](path)` and `![alt](<path>)`. But what about paths like `![alt](path with spaces)` (without angle brackets)? The regex `[^)\s]+` would stop at the space, matching only `path`. This means paths with spaces but without `<>` wrapping would be partially matched. However, the spec says: "Ë∑ØÂæÑÊîØÊåÅÂèØÈÄâÁöÑ <> ÂåÖË£πÂΩ¢Âºè". So paths with spaces REQUIRES `<>` wrapping per the spec. The parser's behavior is correct ‚Äî it will fail to find the full path, and then since the partial path doesn't exist on disk, it will report an error. This is expected behavior per the spec.

> Summary:

>    - M4: Asset library (manual/auto scanning, AssetPanel thumbnails, right-click insert reference)
>    - M5: Preview request (4-section preview doc, shared request body construction with generate)
>    - M6: Prompt injection (modelInjections config, seed injection, IMAGES.md, edit UI)
>    - M7: UX polish (instant card creation, auto-switch tab, timer, progress bar, history merge, MD following editor)
>    - M8: Robustness (runtime validation, transient retry, cleanup, re-entry lock, test completion)
>    - Final verification: Run all checks (`compile`, `check-types`, `lint`, `test`) confirming all green
>    - Code review: Full review against spec, identify issues, fix high-priority ones, re-verify all green

> 2. Key Technical Concepts:
>    - VS Code Extension API (activate/deactivate, WebviewView, commands, globalState, secrets, workspaceState)
>    - TypeScript + esbuild dual build (CommonJS extension + IIFE webview)
>    - React 19 + Radix UI for sidebar webview
>    - postMessage protocol between extension and webview (FrontendMessage/BackendMessage)
>    - Grsai API: POST /v1/api/generate (async), GET /v1/api/result (polling)
>    - Async task management: single 4s timer, re-entry lock, globalState persistence, session-based timeout (10min)
>    - Mock fetch testing via globalThis.fetch override for runtime validation tests
>    - CSP with nonce for webview security
>    - asWebviewUri for local image display in webview

> 3. Files and Code Sections:
> 
>    - **`src/extension.ts`** (final state after all milestones)
>      - Entry point: creates TaskManager + SidebarProvider, registers commands (generateImage, previewRequest), resumes tasks on activation
>      - previewRequest implemented with full flow: readConfig ‚Üí parseMarkdown ‚Üí assemblePrompt ‚Üí buildRequestBody ‚Üí showPreview
>      ```typescript
>      import * as vscode from 'vscode';
>      import * as path from 'path';
>      import * as fs from 'fs';
>      import { parseMarkdown } from './markdown-parser';
>      import { buildRequestBody } from './api';
>      import { seedModelInjections, readConfig } from './config';
>      import { TaskManager } from './task-manager';
>      import { SidebarProvider } from './sidebar-provider';
>      import { assemblePrompt } from './prompt-injection';
>      import { showPreview } from './preview';
> 
>      export function activate(context: vscode.ExtensionContext) {
>        void seedModelInjections(context);
>        const taskManager = new TaskManager(context);
>        const sidebarProvider = new SidebarProvider(context, taskManager);
>        context.subscriptions.push(
>          vscode.window.registerWebviewViewProvider('image-flow.sidebar', sidebarProvider)
>        );
>        taskManager.resume();
>        // ... commands for generateImage, previewRequest registered
>        context.subscriptions.push(taskManager);
>      }
>      ```
> 
>    - **`src/task-manager.ts`** (382 lines, full async task lifecycle)
>      - Key types: JobState (index, id, status, progress, error, transientRetries), TaskState (folderName, mdDirectory, mdBaseName, model, startTime, sessionStartTime, jobs, downloadedImages)
>      - submit(): creates folder ‚Üí creates N jobs (submitting) ‚Üí persists ‚Üí emitUpdate(true) for autoSwitch ‚Üí starts polling ‚Üí background submitJob for each
>      - pollAll(): re-entry lock, iterates tasks, checks timeout, queries running jobs, downloads on success, handles transient errors (keeps running)
>      - finishTask(): cleanup, notifications, delete task, stop polling if empty
>      - failTask(): same cleanup for all-submission-failed case
>      - calcProgress(): weighted average (submitting=0, running=progress, terminal=100)
>      - emitUpdate(autoSwitch): maps TaskState ‚Üí TaskCardInfo[], passes to _onUpdate callback
>      - Resume: restores from globalState, marks submitting‚Üífailed, resets sessionStartTime
>      - MAX_RETRY = 5 for submitJob retries, POLL_INTERVAL = 4000ms, TASK_TIMEOUT = 10min
> 
>    - **`src/sidebar-provider.ts`** (final state, ~288 lines)
>      - Constructor takes TaskManager, binds onUpdate to forward tasks + history to webview
>      - resolveWebviewView: sets view, sets CSP HTML, registers onDidReceiveMessage and onDidChangeActiveTextEditor
>      - handleMessage: routes init/setConfig/setApiKey/addAssetFolder/removeAssetFolder/insertReference/openInBrowser/**generate**/**previewRequest** (last two added during review)
>      - scanHistory(): scans MD dir for task-* folders, excludes active tasks, returns TaskCardInfo[] with isActive=false
>      - buildAssetFolders(): merges manual + auto folders, converts paths to webview URIs
>      - sendAssetUpdate() and sendInitData()
>      - **CRITICAL FIX**: Added generate and previewRequest case handlers that execute VS Code commands
>      - **IMPORTANT FIX**: Added `this._editorListener?.dispose()` before re-registering in resolveWebviewView
>      ```typescript
>      case 'generate': {
>        await vscode.commands.executeCommand('image-flow.generateImage');
>        break;
>      }
>      case 'previewRequest': {
>        await vscode.commands.executeCommand('image-flow.previewRequest');
>        break;
>      }
>      ```
>

>      - getManualFolders/addManualFolder/removeManualFolder: workspaceState persistence

>      rel = path.relative(mdDir, imagePath);  // was path.posix.relative

>    - **`src/api.ts`** (170 lines)
>      - buildRequestBody: model-family-aware body construction (nano-banana: aspectRatio+imageSize, gpt-image-2: aspectRatio only, gpt-image-2-vip: pixel value)
>      - submitGenerate: fetchWithTimeout (30s), validates response, returns job id
>      - queryResult: fetchWithTimeout (30s), validates status enum, returns typed GenerateResponse
>      - isTransientError: ApiError.transient || AbortError || TypeError with fetch/abort
>      - Both functions validate: null body ‚Üí ApiError, empty id ‚Üí ApiError, unknown status ‚Üí ApiError
>

>      - Regex: `/!\[([^\]]*)\]\((<[^>]+>|[^)\s]+)\)/g` ‚Äî handles normal paths and <>-wrapped paths
>      - Deduplicates by path, assigns sequential 1-based indices
>      - Resolves paths relative to mdDir, reads to base64 data URI
>      - Reports errors for missing files, returns errors alongside body and images

> 4. Errors and fixes:
>    - **TypeScript error: 'submitJob' implicitly has return type 'any'**: submitJob calls itself recursively for transient retries, creating a circular reference. Fixed by adding explicit `: Promise<void>` return type annotation.
>    - **ESLint error: 'require-await' on assemblePrompt and resume**: Both were async but had no await. Fixed by removing `async` keyword and changing return type from `Promise<string>` to `string`.
>    - **ESLint error: 'await-thenable' on assemblePrompt call**: After making assemblePrompt sync, the `await` in task-manager.ts line 89 caused a lint error. Fixed by removing `await`.
>    - **ESLint error: 'no-floating-promises' on submitJob call**: Background submitJob calls needed `void` prefix.
>    - **ESLint error: 'no-meaningless-void-operator' on sync sendAssetUpdate**: After making sendAssetUpdate sync, `void` prefix was no longer needed. Removed.
>    - **Test failure: preview test truncation mismatch**: Data URI was 84 chars, slice(0,48) produced "...xyz" but test expected "...stu". Fixed by using shorter, predictable test data URI.
>    - **TypeScript error: 'readonly []' not assignable to 'string[]'**: Used `as const` on test base object making arrays readonly. Fixed by using a `makeReq()` factory function with explicit `as string[]` type.
>    - **CRITICAL bug found during review: Sidebar Generate/Preview buttons silently fail**: The generate and previewRequest FrontendMessage types were defined but never handled in sidebar-provider.ts's handleMessage switch. Fixed by adding case handlers that call `vscode.commands.executeCommand`.
>    - **IMPORTANT bug found during review: _editorListener leaks on resolveWebviewView re-entrance**: Each call to resolveWebviewView would create a new onDidChangeActiveTextEditor listener without disposing the old one. Fixed by adding `this._editorListener?.dispose()` before registering new one.
>    - **Windows compatibility bug in imageRelativeRef**: Used `path.posix.relative` which doesn't handle Windows backslash paths. Fixed by changing to `path.relative` (native).
> 
> 5. Problem Solving:
>    - Successfully implemented all 8 milestones (M1-M8) from the implementation plan, with 9 commits on branch feature/m1-scaffolding
>    - All 29 tests pass, types/lint/compile all green
>    - During code review, identified and fixed 3 bugs (1 critical, 2 important)
>    - 3 additional issues identified but not yet fixed: task thumbnail webview URIs, effective MD not updated on generate, Map iteration safety

If an invoked skill turns out to be wrong for the situation, you don't need to use it.\n\n```dot\ndigraph skill_flow {\n    \"User message received\" [shape=doublecircle];\n    \"About to EnterPlanMode?\" [shape=doublecircle];\n    \"Already brainstormed?\" [shape=diamond];\n    \"Invoke brainstorming skill\" [shape=box];\n    \"Might any skill apply?\" [shape=diamond];\n    \"Invoke Skill tool\" [shape=box];\n    \"Announce: 'Using [skill] to [purpose]'\" [shape=box];\n    \"Has checklist?\" [shape=diamond];\n    \"Create TodoWrite todo per item\" [shape=box];\n    \"Follow skill exactly\" [shape=box];\n    \"Respond (including clarifications)\" [shape=doublecircle];\n\n    \"About to EnterPlanMode?\" -> \"Already brainstormed?\";\n    \"Already brainstormed?\" -> \"Invoke brainstorming skill\" [label=\"no\"];\n    \"Already brainstormed?\" -> \"Might any skill apply?\" [label=\"yes\"];\n    \"Invoke brainstorming skill\" -> \"Might any skill apply?\";\n\n    \"User message received\" -> \"Might any skill apply?\";\n    \"Might any skill apply?\" -> \"Invoke Skill tool\" [label=\"yes, even 1%\"];\n    \"Might any skill apply?\" -> \"Respond (including clarifications)\" [label=\"definitely not\"];\n    \"Invoke Skill tool\" -> \"Announce: 'Using [skill] to [purpose]'\";\n    \"Announce: 'Using [skill] to [purpose]'\" -> \"Has checklist?\";\n    \"Has checklist?\" -> \"Create TodoWrite todo per item\" [label=\"yes\"];\n    \"Has checklist?\" -> \"Follow skill exactly\" [label=\"no\"];\n    \"Create TodoWrite todo per item\" -> \"Follow skill exactly\";\n}\n```\n\n## Red Flags\n\nThese thoughts mean STOP‚Äîyou're rationalizing:\n\n| Thought | Reality |\n|---------|---------|\n| \"This is just a simple question\" | Questions are tasks. ### Hook ¬∑ SessionStart

`02:20:07`

ü™ù Hook ¬∑ `SessionStart`

````
<EXTREMELY_IMPORTANT>
You have superpowers.

**Below is the full content of your 'superpowers:using-superpowers' skill - your introduction to using skills. For all other skills, use the 'Skill' tool:**

---
name: using-superpowers
description: Use when starting any conversation - establishes how to find and use skills, requiring Skill tool invocation before ANY response including clarifying questions
---

<SUBAGENT-STOP>
If you were dispatched as a subagent to execute a specific task, skip this skill.
</SUBAGENT-STOP>

<EXTREMELY-IMPORTANT>
If you think there is even a 1% chance a skill might apply to what you are doing, you ABSOLUTELY MUST invoke the skill.

IF A SKILL APPLIES TO YOUR TASK, YOU DO NOT HAVE A CHOICE. YOU MUST USE IT.

This is not negotiable. This is not optional. You cannot rationalize your way out of this.
</EXTREMELY-IMPORTANT>

## Instruction Priority

Superpowers skills override default system prompt behavior, but **user instructions always take precedence**:

1. **User's explicit instructions** (CLAUDE.md, GEMINI.md, AGENTS.md, direct requests) ‚Äî highest priority
2. **Superpowers skills** ‚Äî override default system behavior where they conflict
3. **Default system prompt** ‚Äî lowest priority

If CLAUDE.md, GEMINI.md, or AGENTS.md says "don't use TDD" and a skill says "always use TDD," follow the user's instructions. The user is in control.

## How to Access Skills

**In Claude Code:** Use the `Skill` tool. When you invoke a skill, its content is loaded and presented to you‚Äîfollow it directly. Never use the Read tool on skill files.

**In Copilot CLI:** Use the `skill` tool. Skills are auto-discovered from installed plugins. The `skill` tool works the same as Claude Code's `Skill` tool.

**In Gemini CLI:** Skills activate via the `activate_skill` tool. Gemini loads skill metadata at session start and activates the full content on demand.

**In other environments:** Check your platform's documentation for how skills are loaded.

## Platform Adaptation

Skills use Claude Code tool names. Non-CC platforms: see `references/copilot-tools.md` (Copilot CLI), `references/codex-tools.md` (Codex) for tool equivalents. Gemini CLI users get the tool mapping loaded automatically via GEMINI.md.

# Using Skills

## The Rule

**Invoke relevant or requested skills BEFORE any response or action.** Even a 1% chance a skill might apply means that you should invoke the skill to check. If an invoked skill turns out to be wrong for the situation, you don't need to use it.

```dot
digraph skill_flow {
    "User message received" [shape=doublecircle];
    "About to EnterPlanMode?" [shape=doublecircle];
    "Already brainstormed?" [shape=diamond];
    "Invoke brainstorming skill" [shape=box];
    "Might any skill apply?" [shape=diamond];
    "Invoke Skill tool" [shape=box];
    "Announce: 'Using [skill] to [purpose]'" [shape=box];
    "Has checklist?" [shape=diamond];
    "Create TodoWrite todo per item" [shape=box];
    "Follow skill exactly" [shape=box];
    "Respond (including clarifications)" [shape=doublecircle];

    "About to EnterPlanMode?" -> "Already brainstormed?";
    "Already brainstormed?" -> "Invoke brainstorming skill" [label="no"];
    "Already brainstormed?" -> "Might any skill apply?" [label="yes"];
    "Invoke brainstorming skill" -> "Might any skill apply?";

    "User message received" -> "Might any skill apply?";
    "Might any skill apply?" -> "Invoke Skill tool" [label="yes, even 1%"];
    "Might any skill apply?" -> "Respond (including clarifications)" [label="definitely not"];
    "Invoke Skill tool" -> "Announce: 'Using [skill] to [purpose]'";
    "Announce: 'Using [skill] to [purpose]'" -> "Has checklist?";
    "Has checklist?" -> "Create TodoWrite todo per item" [label="yes"];
    "Has checklist?" -> "Follow skill exactly" [label="no"];
    "Create TodoWrite todo per item" -> "Follow skill exactly";
}
```

## Red Flags

These thoughts mean STOP‚Äîyou're rationalizing:

| Thought | Reality |
|---------|---------|
| "This is just a simple question" | Questions are tasks. Check for skills. |
| "I need more context first" | Skill check comes BEFORE clarifying questions. |
| "Let me explore the codebase first" | Skills tell you HOW to explore. Check first. |
| "I can check git/files quickly" | Files lack conversation context. Check for skills. |
| "Let me gather information first" | Skills tell you HOW to gather information. |
| "This doesn't need a formal skill" | If a skill exists, use it. |
| "I remember this skill" | Skills evolve. Read current version. |
| "This doesn't count as a task" | Action = task. Check for skills. |
| "The skill is overkill" | Simple things become complex. Use it. |
| "I'll just do this one thing first" | Check BEFORE doing anything. |
| "This feels productive" | Undisciplined action wastes time. Skills prevent this. |
| "I know what that means" | Knowing the concept ‚âÝ using the skill. Invoke it. |

## Skill Priority

When multiple skills could apply, use this order:

1. **Process skills first** (brainstorming, debugging) - these determine HOW to approach the task
2. **Implementation skills second** (frontend-design, mcp-builder) - these guide execution

"Let's build X" ‚Üí brainstorming first, then implementation skills.
"Fix this bug" ‚Üí debugging first, then domain-specific skills.

## Skill Types

**Rigid** (TDD, debugging): Follow exactly. Don't adapt away discipline.

**Flexible** (patterns): Adapt principles to context.

The skill itself tells you which.

## User Instructions

Instructions say WHAT, not HOW. "Add X" or "Fix Y" doesn't mean skip workflows.


</EXTREMELY_IMPORTANT>
````

<details>
<summary><code>/home/particle/2026-1/harness-human-research/image-flow/runs/eval-manual-superpowers-260611093413/src/task-manager.ts</code></summary>

```typescript
import * as vscode from 'vscode';
import * as path from 'path';
import * as fs from 'fs';
import { parseMarkdown } from './markdown-parser';
import { submitGenerate, queryResult, isTransientError } from './api';
import { extFromUrl } from './image-utils';
import { assemblePrompt } from './prompt-injection';
import { readConfig } from './config';
import type { TaskCardInfo } from './shared';

const STORAGE_KEY = 'runningTasks';
const POLL_INTERVAL = 4000;
const TASK_TIMEOUT = 10 * 60 * 1000; // 10 ÂàÜÈíü
const MAX_RETRY = 5;

interface JobState {
  index: number;
  id: string;
  status: 'submitting' | 'running' | 'succeeded' | 'failed' | 'violation';
  progress: number;
  error: string;
  transientRetries: number;
}

interface TaskState {
  folderName: string;
  mdDirectory: string;
  mdBaseName: string;
  model: string;
  startTime: number;
  sessionStartTime: number;
  jobs: JobState[];
  downloadedImages: string[];
}

export class TaskManager {
  private tasks: Map<string, TaskState> = new Map();
  private timer: ReturnType<typeof setInterval> | null = null;
  private polling = false;
  private _onUpdate?: (tasks: TaskCardInfo[], autoSwitch?: boolean) => void;

  constructor(private readonly context: vscode.ExtensionContext) {}

  set onUpdate(cb: (tasks: TaskCardInfo[], autoSwitch?: boolean) => void) { this._onUpdate = cb; }

  /** ÊÅ¢Â§çÊåÅ‰πÖÂåñÁöÑËøõË°å‰∏≠‰ªªÂä° */
  resume() {
    const stored = this.context.globalState.get<TaskState[]>(STORAGE_KEY) ?? [];
    const now = Date.now();
    for (const task of stored) {
      task.sessionStartTime = now;
      for (const job of task.jobs) {
        if (job.status === 'submitting') {
          job.status = 'failed';
          job.error = 'Êèê‰∫§Êú™ÂÆåÊàêÔºàÊâ©Â±ïÂ∑≤ÂÖ≥Èó≠Ôºâ';
        }
      }
      this.tasks.set(task.folderName, task);
    }
    this.emitUpdate();
    if (this.tasks.size > 0) {
      this.startPolling();
    }
  }

  private persist() {
    const arr = Array.from(this.tasks.values());
    void this.context.globalState.update(STORAGE_KEY, arr);
  }

  /** Êèê‰∫§Êñ∞‰ªªÂä° */
  async submit(mdUri: vscode.Uri): Promise<string> {
    const config = await readConfig(this.context);
    if (!config.apiKey) {
      throw new Error('ËØ∑ÂÖàËÆæÁΩÆ API Key');
    }

    const raw = fs.readFileSync(mdUri.fsPath, 'utf-8');
    if (raw.trim() === '') {
      throw new Error('ÂÜÖÂÆπ‰∏∫Á©∫');
    }

    const mdDir = path.dirname(mdUri.fsPath);
    const { body, images, errors: parseErrors } = parseMarkdown(raw, mdDir);
    if (parseErrors.length > 0) {
      throw new Error(`ÂèÇËÄÉÂõæËØªÂèñÂ§±Ë¥•:\n${parseErrors.join('\n')}`);
    }

    const finalPrompt = assemblePrompt(config.model, config.modelInjections, body, mdDir);

    const folderName = createTaskFolder(mdDir);

    const now = Date.now();
    const N = Math.min(config.concurrency, 10);
    const jobs: JobState[] = [];
    for (let i = 1; i <= N; i++) {
      jobs.push({
        index: i,
        id: '',
        status: 'submitting',
        progress: 0,
        error: '',
        transientRetries: 0,
      });
    }

    const task: TaskState = {
      folderName,
      mdDirectory: mdDir,
      mdBaseName: path.basename(mdUri.fsPath, '.md'),
      model: config.model,
      startTime: now,
      sessionStartTime: now,
      jobs,
      downloadedImages: [],
    };

    // Á´ãÂç≥Âª∫Âç°ËøîÂõûÔºåautoSwitch ‰øÉ‰ΩøÂâçÁ´ØÂàáÂà∞‰ªªÂä°È°µ
    this.tasks.set(folderName, task);
    this.persist();
    this.emitUpdate(true);
    this.startPolling();

    // ÂêéÂè∞Âπ∂ÂèëÂèëÂá∫ N ‰∏™ generate ËØ∑Ê±Ç
    const refImages = images.map(i => i.dataUri);
    for (const job of jobs) {
      void this.submitJob(task, job, finalPrompt, refImages, config);
    }

    return folderName;
  }

  private async submitJob(
    task: TaskState,
    job: JobState,
    prompt: string,
    refImages: string[],
    config: Awaited<ReturnType<typeof readConfig>>
  ): Promise<void> {
    try {
      const jobId = await submitGenerate({
        baseUrl: config.baseUrl,
        apiKey: config.apiKey,
        model: config.model,
        prompt,
        images: refImages,
        aspectRatio: config.aspectRatio,
        imageSize: config.imageSize,
      });
      job.id = jobId;
      job.status = 'running';
      job.transientRetries = 0;
    } catch (err) {
      if (isTransientError(err) && job.transientRetries < MAX_RETRY) {
        job.transientRetries++;
        this.persist();
        await this.submitJob(task, job, prompt, refImages, config);
        return;
      }
      job.status = 'failed';
      job.error = err instanceof Error ? err.message : String(err);
    }
    this.persist();
    this.emitUpdate();

    // Ê£ÄÊü•ÊòØÂê¶ÂÖ®ÈÉ®Êèê‰∫§Â§±Ë¥•
    const allDone = task.jobs.every(j => j.status !== 'submitting');
    if (allDone) {
      const allFailed = task.jobs.every(j => j.status === 'failed');
      if (allFailed) {
        this.failTask(task);
      }
    }
  }

  private startPolling() {
    if (this.timer) return;
    this.timer = setInterval(() => {
      void this.pollAll();
    }, POLL_INTERVAL);
  }

  private stopPolling() {
    if (this.timer) {
      clearInterval(this.timer);
      this.timer = null;
    }
  }

  private async pollAll() {
    if (this.polling) return;
    this.polling = true;
    try {
      const config = await readConfig(this.context);
      const now = Date.now();

      for (const task of this.tasks.values()) {
        // Ë∂ÖÊó∂ÂÖúÂ∫ï
        if (now - task.sessionStartTime > TASK_TIMEOUT) {
          for (const job of task.jobs) {
            if (job.status === 'running' || job.status === 'submitting') {
              job.status = 'failed';
              job.error = '‰ªªÂä°Ë∂ÖÊó∂Ôºà10 ÂàÜÈíüÔºâ';
            }
          }
          this.finishTask(task);
          continue;
        }

        let allDone = true;
        for (const job of task.jobs) {
          if (job.status === 'running') {
            allDone = false;
            try {
              const result = await queryResult(config.baseUrl, config.apiKey, job.id);
              job.progress = result.progress ?? job.progress;

              if (result.status === 'succeeded' && result.results) {
                const folderPath = path.join(task.mdDirectory, task.folderName);
                for (const r of result.results) {
                  const seq = task.downloadedImages.length + 1;
                  const ext = extFromUrl(r.url);
                  const filename = `${task.mdBaseName}-${seq}.${ext}`;
                  const dest = path.join(folderPath, filename);
                  await downloadFile(r.url, dest);
                  task.downloadedImages.push(filename);
                }
                job.status = 'succeeded';
                job.progress = 100;
                job.transientRetries = 0;
              } else if (result.status === 'failed' || result.status === 'violation') {
                job.status = result.status;
                job.error = result.error ?? 'ÁîüÊàêÂ§±Ë¥•';
              }
            } catch (err) {
              if (isTransientError(err)) {
                allDone = false;
              } else {
                job.status = 'failed';
                job.error = err instanceof Error ? err.message : String(err);
              }
            }
          }

          if (job.status === 'submitting') {
            allDone = false;
          }
        }

        if (allDone) {
          this.finishTask(task);
        }
      }
      this.persist();
    } finally {
      this.polling = false;
    }
  }

  /** ‰ªªÂä°ÁªàÁªìÂ§ÑÁêÜ */
  private finishTask(task: TaskState) {
    const successCount = task.jobs.filter(j => j.status === 'succeeded').length;
    const failedJobs = task.jobs.filter(j => j.status === 'failed' || j.status === 'violation');
    const errors = [...new Set(failedJobs.map(j => j.error).filter(Boolean))];

    if (successCount === 0) {
      const folderPath = path.join(task.mdDirectory, task.folderName);
      try {
        if (fs.readdirSync(folderPath).length === 0) {
          fs.rmdirSync(folderPath);
        }
      } catch { /* ÂøΩÁï• */ }

      if (errors.length > 0) {
        vscode.window.showErrorMessage(
          `‰ªªÂä° ${task.folderName} ÂÖ®ÈÉ®Â§±Ë¥•: ${errors.join('; ')}`
        );
      }
    } else if (failedJobs.length > 0) {
      vscode.window.showWarningMessage(
        `‰ªªÂä° ${task.folderName} ÂÆåÊàê ${successCount}/${task.jobs.length}Ôºå${failedJobs.length} ÂºÝÂ§±Ë¥•: ${errors.join('; ')}`
      );
    }

    this.tasks.delete(task.folderName);
    this.persist();
    this.emitUpdate();

    if (this.tasks.size === 0) {
      this.stopPolling();
    }
  }

  /** ÂÖ®ÈÉ®Êèê‰∫§Â§±Ë¥•‚Äî‚ÄîÊï¥‰ªªÂä°‰ΩúÂ∫ü */
  private failTask(task: TaskState) {
    const folderPath = path.join(task.mdDirectory, task.folderName);
    try {
      if (fs.readdirSync(folderPath).length === 0) {
        fs.rmdirSync(folderPath);
      }
    } catch { /* ÂøΩÁï• */ }

    const errors = [...new Set(task.jobs.map(j => j.error).filter(Boolean))];
    if (errors.length > 0) {
      vscode.window.showErrorMessage(
        `‰ªªÂä°Êèê‰∫§ÂÖ®ÈÉ®Â§±Ë¥•: ${errors.join('; ')}`
      );
    }

    this.tasks.delete(task.folderName);
    this.persist();
    this.emitUpdate();

    if (this.tasks.size === 0) {
      this.stopPolling();
    }
  }

  /** ËÆ°ÁÆóËÅöÂêàËøõÂ∫¶ */
  private calcProgress(task: TaskState): number {
    const N = task.jobs.length;
    if (N === 0) return 0;
    let total = 0;
    for (const job of task.jobs) {
      if (job.status === 'succeeded' || job.status === 'failed' || job.status === 'violation') {
        total += 100;
      } else if (job.status === 'running') {
        total += job.progress;
      }
    }
    return Math.round(total / N);
  }

  /** ÂèëÂ∞Ñ‰ªªÂä°Âç°ÂàóË°®ÁªôÂâçÁ´Ø */
  private emitUpdate(autoSwitch = false) {
    if (!this._onUpdate) return;
    const cards: TaskCardInfo[] = Array.from(this.tasks.values()).map(t => ({
      folderName: t.folderName,
      model: t.model,
      status: t.jobs.every(j => j.status === 'submitting') ? 'submitting' :
              t.jobs.every(j => j.status === 'succeeded') ? 'done' : 'running',
      jobCount: t.jobs.length,
      doneCount: t.jobs.filter(j => j.status === 'succeeded').length,
      failedCount: t.jobs.filter(j => j.status === 'failed' || j.status === 'violation').length,
      progress: this.calcProgress(t),
      startTime: t.startTime,
      downloadedImages: t.downloadedImages,
      errors: t.jobs.map(j => j.error).filter(Boolean),
      isActive: true,
    }));
    cards.sort((a, b) => b.folderName.localeCompare(a.folderName));
    this._onUpdate(cards, autoSwitch);
  }

  dispose() {
    this.stopPolling();
  }
}

// --- Â∑•ÂÖ∑ÂáΩÊï∞ ---

function pad(n: number): string { return String(n).padStart(2, '0'); }

function createTaskFolder(mdDir: string): string {
  const now = new Date();
  const ts = `${String(now.getFullYear()).slice(2)}${pad(now.getMonth() + 1)}${pad(now.getDate())}${pad(now.getHours())}${pad(now.getMinutes())}${pad(now.getSeconds())}`;
  let seq = 0;
  let name: string;
  do {
    seq++;
    name = `task-${ts}-${seq}`;
  } while (fs.existsSync(path.join(mdDir, name)));
  fs.mkdirSync(path.join(mdDir, name));
  return name;
}

async function downloadFile(url: string, dest: string): Promise<void> {
  const res = await fetch(url);
  if (!res.ok) throw new Error(`‰∏ãËΩΩÂ§±Ë¥•: ${res.status}`);
  const buf = Buffer.from(await res.arrayBuffer());
  fs.writeFileSync(dest, buf);
}

```
